hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Hannibalis' — 'Hannibal' is nominative, the subject
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Hannibale' — 'Hannibal' is nominative here

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Hannibalis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Hannibalis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Hannibalis' ends in -is

hints.ablative: 'Ad' always takes the accusative — ablative prepositions include 'ab', 'ex', 'cum'
hints.genitive: 'Ad' takes accusative — no common Latin preposition governs genitive
hints.dative: 'Ad' takes accusative — the dative is not governed by prepositions in Latin

steps.reveal: portas = the gates (acc. pl., governed by 'ad')

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'portae' — 'portas' with -as is accusative plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'portis' — 'portas' is accusative after 'ad'
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'portarum' — 'portas' is accusative

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'portae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'portae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'portae' with -ae is 1st declension

steps.prompt: What verb is understood/implied in this famous phrase?

hints.venit (comes): While Hannibal did 'come', the implied verb in this exclamation is 'est' — Hannibal IS at the gates
hints.stat (stands): While vivid, the traditional understood verb is 'est' — a simple statement of presence
hints.pugnat (fights): The phrase expresses alarm at Hannibal's presence, not fighting — 'est' is understood

steps.prompt: The infinitive of the implied 'est' translates to?

hints.to go: That would be 'ire' — the implied 'est' comes from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to stand: That would be 'stare' — the implied 'est' comes from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to come: That would be 'venire' — the implied 'est' comes from 'esse' (to be)

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.venio, venire, veni, ventum: These are the parts of 'venire' (to come)
